revision.comment: 新しいページ：竹葉瀬。
revision.text:
'''竹葉瀬'''（たかはせ<ref>読みは『日本古代氏族人名辞典』竹葉瀬項による。</ref>）は、[[古墳時代]]の[[人物]]。'''多奇波世'''、'''竹合'''とも。また氏の名を冠して'''上毛野竹葉瀬'''、'''上毛野竹合'''とも。
[[上毛野氏|上毛野君]]の祖。[[仁徳天皇]]年間に[[朝鮮]]へ派遣されたという[[武将]]である。
== 事績 == 『[[日本書紀]]』[[仁徳天皇]]53年5月条によると、[[新羅]]に派遣され、新羅が朝貢しないことを問責したという。その途上では、白鹿を獲たので天皇に献上したのち新羅へ向かったと記される。また、のちに新羅と戦った[[上毛野田道]]の兄という。
== 後裔氏族 == 『[[新撰姓氏録]]』には、以下の氏族が後裔として記載されている。 * 皇別 左京 上毛野朝臣 - 下毛野朝臣同祖。豊城入彦命五世孫・多奇波世君の後。（以下略）。 * 皇別 左京 住吉朝臣 - 上毛野同祖。豊城入彦命五世孫・多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 池原朝臣 - 住吉同氏。多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 桑原公 - 上毛野同祖。豊城入彦命五世孫・多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 川合公 - 上毛野同氏。多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 商長首 - 上毛野同氏。多奇波世君の後。三世孫・久比、（以下略）。
『[[日本書紀私記|日本書紀弘仁私記]]』序には「諸蕃雑姓」に注として、 : 「田辺史、上毛野公、池原朝臣、住吉朝臣らの祖、思須美（しすみ）、和徳（わとく）の両人、大鷦鷯天皇（おおさざき-、[[仁徳天皇]]）御宇の年、百済国より化来す。しかして言うに、おのれらの祖、これ貴国将軍上野公竹合（たかはせ）なりといえり。天皇、矜憐して彼の族に混ず。しかして、この書に諸蕃人（= 渡来人）というなり。」
とあり<ref name="再発見">熊倉浩靖「上毛野国（かみつけのくに）から東国（あづま）へ」（『群馬史再発見』あさを社、2001年）。</ref>、田辺史・上毛野公・池原朝臣・住吉朝臣4氏が百済からの渡来人でありながら、竹葉瀬の後裔を仮冒したことがわかる。
== 考証 == 『日本書紀弘仁私記』にあるように、竹葉瀬を祖と仮冒する渡来人グループが形成されていた。ここから、上毛野氏が文化・軍事両面において対朝鮮の交渉に携わっていたことが示唆される<ref name="再発見"/>。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本古代氏族人名辞典』（[[吉川弘文館]]、2010年普及版）竹葉瀬項
== 関連項目 == * [[毛野氏]] ** [[上毛野氏]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:たかはせ}} [[Category:古墳時代の人物]] [[Category:毛野氏]]

'''竹葉瀬'''（たかはせ<ref>読みは『日本古代氏族人名辞典』竹葉瀬項による。</ref>）は、[[古墳時代]]に活躍した渡来系の[[人物]]。'''多奇波世'''、'''竹合'''とも。また氏の名を冠して'''上毛野竹葉瀬'''、'''上毛野竹合'''とも。
[[日本書紀]]及び[[古事記]]において皇族とされる[[上毛野氏|上毛野君]]の祖。[[仁徳天皇]]年間に[[朝鮮]]へ派遣されたという[[武将]]である。
== 事績 == 『[[日本書紀]]』[[仁徳天皇]]53年5月条によると、[[新羅]]に派遣され、新羅が朝貢しないことを問責したという。その途上では、白鹿を獲たので天皇に献上したのち新羅へ向かったと記される。また、のちに新羅と戦った[[上毛野田道]]の兄という。
== 後裔氏族 == 『[[新撰姓氏録]]』には、以下の氏族が後裔として記載されている。 * 皇別 左京 上毛野朝臣 - 下毛野朝臣同祖。豊城入彦命五世孫・多奇波世君の後。（以下略）。 * 皇別 左京 住吉朝臣 - 上毛野同祖。豊城入彦命五世孫・多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 池原朝臣 - 住吉同氏。多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 桑原公 - 上毛野同祖。豊城入彦命五世孫・多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 川合公 - 上毛野同氏。多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 商長首 - 上毛野同氏。多奇波世君の後。三世孫・久比、（以下略）。

とあり<ref name="再発見">熊倉浩靖「上毛野国（かみつけのくに）から東国（あづま）へ」（『群馬史再発見』あさを社、2001年）。</ref>、田辺史・上毛野公・池原朝臣・住吉朝臣4氏が百済からの渡来人でありながら、竹葉瀬の後裔を仮冒したことがわかる。

'''竹葉瀬'''（たかはせ{{Sfn|竹葉瀬（古代氏族）|1979年}}）は、[[古墳時代]]の[[人物]]。表記は「'''多奇波世'''」「'''竹合'''」とも。また氏の名を冠して「上毛野竹葉瀬」「上毛野竹合」とも。

revision.comment: /* 関連項目 */ [[Category:古代日朝関係]]追加。
revision.text:
'''竹葉瀬'''（たかはせ{{Sfn|竹葉瀬（古代氏族）|1979年}}）は、『[[日本書紀]]』等に伝わる古代[[日本]]の[[人物]]。表記は「'''多奇波世'''」「'''竹合'''」とも。また氏の名を冠して「上毛野竹葉瀬」「上毛野竹合」とも。
[[上毛野氏|上毛野君]]の祖で、[[仁徳天皇]]（第16代）の時に[[朝鮮]]へ派遣されたという[[武将]]である。
== 記録 == 『[[日本書紀]]』[[仁徳天皇]]53年5月条によると、[[新羅]]に派遣され、新羅が朝貢しないことを問責したという。その途上では、白鹿を獲たので天皇に献上したのち新羅へ向かったと記される。また、のちに新羅と戦った[[上毛野田道]]の兄という。
== 後裔氏族 == 『[[新撰姓氏録]]』では、次の氏族が後裔として記載されている。 * 皇別 左京 上毛野朝臣 - 下毛野朝臣同祖。豊城入彦命五世孫・多奇波世君の後。（以下略）。 * 皇別 左京 住吉朝臣 - 上毛野同祖。豊城入彦命五世孫・多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 池原朝臣 - 住吉同氏。多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 桑原公 - 上毛野同祖。豊城入彦命五世孫・多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 川合公 - 上毛野同氏。多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 商長首 - 上毛野同氏。多奇波世君の後。三世孫・久比、（以下略）。
『[[日本書紀私記|日本書紀弘仁私記]]』序には「諸蕃雑姓」に注として、 : 「田辺史、上毛野公、池原朝臣、住吉朝臣らの祖、思須美（しすみ）、和徳（わとく）の両人、大鷦鷯天皇（おおさざき-、[[仁徳天皇]]）御宇の年、百済国より化来す。しかして言うに、おのれらの祖、これ貴国将軍上野公竹合（たかはせ）なりといえり。天皇、矜憐して彼の族に混ず。しかして、この書に諸蕃人（= 渡来人）というなり。」
とあり<ref name="再発見">熊倉浩靖「上毛野国（かみつけのくに）から東国（あづま）へ」（『群馬史再発見』あさを社、2001年）。</ref>、田辺史・上毛野公・池原朝臣・住吉朝臣4氏が百済からの渡来人でありながら、竹葉瀬の後裔を仮冒したことがわかる。
== 考証 == 『日本書紀弘仁私記』にあるように、竹葉瀬を祖と仮冒する渡来人グループが形成されていた。ここから、上毛野氏が文化・軍事両面において対朝鮮の交渉に携わっていたことが示唆される<ref name="再発見"/>。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=竹葉瀬|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=9784642014588|ref={{Harvid|竹葉瀬（古代氏族）|1979年}}}}
== 関連項目 == * [[上毛野氏]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:たかはせ}} [[Category:古墳時代の人物]] [[Category:上毛野氏|伝たかはせ]] [[Category:古代日朝関係]]

revision.text:
{{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 竹葉瀬 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[古墳時代]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = 竹葉瀬命、多奇波世君 | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = [[上毛野国造]] | 主君 = [[仁徳天皇]] | 氏族 = [[上毛野氏|上毛野君]] | 父母 = [[大荒田別命]]<ref name="nakata">[[中田憲信]]『皇胤志』（[[宝賀寿男]]「第2章 皇族系氏族 第8節 毛野氏族 1上毛野君」『古代氏族系譜集成』上巻、古代氏族研究会、1986年、558、559頁。）。</ref> | 兄弟 = [[鹿我別命]]<ref name="nakata"/>、[[現古命]]<ref name="nakata"/>、[[奈良別君]]<ref name="nakata"/>、[[田道君]]<ref name="nakata"/>、[[賀表乃眞稚命]]<ref name="nakata"/> | 妻 = 呉女<ref name="nakata"/> | 子 = 特君（持君）<ref name="nakata"/> | 特記事項 = }} '''竹葉瀬'''（たかはせ{{Sfn|竹葉瀬（古代氏族）|2010年}}、[[生没年不詳]]）は、『[[日本書紀]]』等に伝わる古代[[日本]]の[[人物]]で初代'''[[上毛野国造]]'''。表記は'''竹葉瀬命'''、'''多奇波世君'''、'''竹合'''とも。
[[上毛野氏|上毛野君]]の祖で、[[仁徳天皇]]（第16代）の時に[[朝鮮]]へ派遣されたという[[武将]]である。
== 記録 == 『[[日本書紀]]』[[仁徳天皇]]53年5月条によると、[[新羅]]に派遣され、新羅が朝貢しないことを問責したという。その途上では、白鹿を獲たので天皇に献上したのち新羅へ向かったと記される。また、のちに新羅と戦った[[上毛野田道]]の兄という。
== 後裔氏族 == 『[[新撰姓氏録]]』では、次の氏族が後裔として記載されている。 * 皇別 左京 上毛野朝臣 - 下毛野朝臣同祖。豊城入彦命五世孫・多奇波世君の後。（以下略）。 * 皇別 左京 住吉朝臣 - 上毛野同祖。豊城入彦命五世孫・多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 池原朝臣 - 住吉同氏。多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 桑原公 - 上毛野同祖。豊城入彦命五世孫・多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 川合公 - 上毛野同氏。多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 商長首 - 上毛野同氏。多奇波世君の後。三世孫・久比、（以下略）。
『[[日本書紀私記|日本書紀弘仁私記]]』序には「諸蕃雑姓」に注として、 : 「田辺史、上毛野公、池原朝臣、住吉朝臣らの祖、思須美（しすみ）、和徳（わとく）の両人、大鷦鷯天皇（おおさざき-、[[仁徳天皇]]）御宇の年、百済国より化来す。しかして言うに、おのれらの祖、これ貴国将軍上野公竹合（たかはせ）なりといえり。天皇、矜憐して彼の族に混ず。しかして、この書に諸蕃人（= 渡来人）というなり。」
とあり<ref name="再発見">熊倉浩靖「上毛野国（かみつけのくに）から東国（あづま）へ」（『群馬史再発見』あさを社、2001年）。</ref>、田辺史・上毛野公・池原朝臣・住吉朝臣4氏が百済からの渡来人でありながら、竹葉瀬の後裔を仮冒したことがわかる。
== 考証 == 『日本書紀弘仁私記』にあるように、竹葉瀬を祖と仮冒する渡来人グループが形成されていた。ここから、上毛野氏が文化・軍事両面において対朝鮮の交渉に携わっていたことが示唆される<ref name="再発見"/>。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=竹葉瀬|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|竹葉瀬（古代氏族）|2010年}}}}
== 関連項目 == * [[飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] * [[上毛野氏]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:たかはせ}} [[Category:日本神話の人物]] [[Category:古代日本の人物]] [[Category:古墳時代の人物]] [[Category:上野国の人物]] [[Category:国造]] [[Category:上毛野氏|たかはせ]] [[Category:仁徳天皇]] [[Category:古代日朝関係]]

'''竹葉瀬'''（たかはせ{{Sfn|竹葉瀬（古代氏族）|2010年}}）は、『[[日本書紀]]』等に伝わる古代朝鮮系の[[日本]]の[[人物]]。表記は「'''多奇波世'''」「'''竹合'''」とも。また氏の名を冠して「上毛野竹葉瀬」「上毛野竹合」とも。
[[上毛野氏|上毛野君]]の祖で、[[仁徳天皇]]（第16代）の時に[[朝鮮]]へ派遣されたという[[武将]]である。

『[[日本書紀私記|日本書紀弘仁私記]]』序には「諸蕃雑姓」に注として、 : 「田辺史、上毛野公、池原朝臣、住吉朝臣らの祖、思須美（しすみ）、和徳（わとく）の両人、大鷦鷯天皇（おおさざき-、[[仁徳天皇]]）御宇の年、百済国より化来す。しかして言うに、おのれらの祖、これ貴国将軍上野公竹合（たかはせ）なりといえり。天皇、矜憐して彼の族に混ず。しかして、この書に諸蕃人（= 渡来人）というなり。」

revision.text:
'''Takahase''' (竹葉瀬) appears in ancient Japanese texts such as the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as an individual from ancient [[Japan]]. Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}, the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans ==
The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>

'''Takahase''' (竹葉瀬) appears in ancient Japanese texts such as the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as an individual from ancient [[Japan]]. Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}, the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道}}, who later fought against Silla.

The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>

Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>

Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}, the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道}}, who later fought against Silla.

'''Takahase''' (竹葉瀬) appears in ancient Japanese texts such as the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as an individual from ancient [[Japan]]. Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}, the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans ==
The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|lang=ja|chapter=竹葉瀬|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2010|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|竹葉瀬（古代氏族）|2010年}}}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}

revision.text:
'''Takahase''' (竹葉瀬) appears in ancient Japanese texts such as the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as an individual from ancient [[Japan]]. Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}, the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans ==
The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|lang=ja|chapter=竹葉瀬|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2010|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|竹葉瀬（古代氏族）|2010年}}}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}

The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>

'''Takahase''' (竹葉瀬) appears in ancient Japanese texts such as the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as an individual from ancient [[Japan]]. Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}, the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道}}, who later fought against Silla.

Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.

'''Takahase''' (竹葉瀬) appears in ancient Japanese texts such as the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as an individual from ancient [[Japan]]. Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}, the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans ==
The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>

revision.text:
'''Takahase''' (竹葉瀬) appears in ancient Japanese texts such as the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as an individual from ancient [[Japan]]. Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}, the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans ==
The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|lang=ja|chapter=竹葉瀬|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2010|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|竹葉瀬（古代氏族）|2010年}}}}

Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."

revision.comment: Text replacement - "-qq]]" to "—>[[category:qq close]]"
revision.text:
'''Takahase''' (竹葉瀬) appears in ancient Japanese texts such as the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as an individual from ancient [[Japan]]. Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}, the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans ==
The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.

They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Qqqqqqqqqq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Takahase''' (竹葉瀬) appears in ancient Japanese texts such as the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as an individual from ancient [[Japan]]. Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}, the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans ==
The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>
== References == {{reflist}}

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Takahase''' (竹葉瀬) appears in ancient Japanese texts such as the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as an individual from ancient [[Japan]]. Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}, the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans ==
The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|lang=ja|chapter=竹葉瀬|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2010|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|竹葉瀬（古代氏族）|2010年}}}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Takahase''' (竹葉瀬) appears in ancient Japanese texts such as the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as an individual from ancient [[Japan]]. Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}, the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans ==
The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|lang=ja|chapter=竹葉瀬|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2010|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|竹葉瀬（古代氏族）|2010年}}}}

'''Takahase''' (竹葉瀬) appears in ancient Japanese texts such as the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as an individual from ancient [[Japan]]. Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}, the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans ==
The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Bump 1-20 (2)]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Takahase''' (竹葉瀬) appears in ancient Japanese texts such as the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as an individual from ancient [[Japan]]. Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}, the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans ==
The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:return the see also]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Takahase''' (竹葉瀬) appears in ancient Japanese texts such as the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as an individual from ancient [[Japan]]. Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}, the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans ==
The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|lang=ja|chapter=竹葉瀬|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2010|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|竹葉瀬（古代氏族）|2010年}}}}== See Also == * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}

'''Takahase''' (竹葉瀬) appears in ancient Japanese texts such as the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as an individual from ancient [[Japan]]. Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇}}, the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans ==
The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|lang=ja|chapter=竹葉瀬|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|year=2010|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|竹葉瀬（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}

The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記|12=draft}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国|12=draft}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Return the see also]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Takahase''' (竹葉瀬) appears in ancient Japanese texts such as the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}'' as an individual from ancient [[Japan]]. Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=draft}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|12=draft}}, the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅|12=draft}} to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道|12=draft}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans ==
The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft}}'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記|12=draft}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国|12=draft}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>

16 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:竹葉瀬 up to rev 95784019
revision.text:
'''竹葉瀬'''（たかはせ{{Sfn|竹葉瀬（古代氏族）|2010年}}）は、『[[日本書紀]]』等に伝わる古代[[日本]]の[[人物]]。表記は「'''多奇波世'''」「'''竹合'''」とも。また氏の名を冠して「上毛野竹葉瀬」「上毛野竹合」とも。
[[上毛野氏|上毛野君]]の祖で、[[仁徳天皇]]（第16代）の時に[[朝鮮]]へ派遣されたという[[武将]]である。
== 記録 == 『[[日本書紀]]』[[仁徳天皇]]53年5月条によると、[[新羅]]に派遣され、新羅が朝貢しないことを問責したという。その途上では、白鹿を獲たので天皇に献上したのち新羅へ向かったと記される。また、のちに新羅と戦った[[上毛野田道]]の兄という。
== 後裔氏族 == 『[[新撰姓氏録]]』では、次の氏族が後裔として記載されている。 * 皇別 左京 上毛野朝臣 - 下毛野朝臣同祖。豊城入彦命五世孫・多奇波世君の後。（以下略）。 * 皇別 左京 住吉朝臣 - 上毛野同祖。豊城入彦命五世孫・多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 池原朝臣 - 住吉同氏。多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 桑原公 - 上毛野同祖。豊城入彦命五世孫・多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 川合公 - 上毛野同氏。多奇波世君の後。 * 皇別 左京 商長首 - 上毛野同氏。多奇波世君の後。三世孫・久比、（以下略）。
『[[日本書紀私記|日本書紀弘仁私記]]』序には「諸蕃雑姓」に注として、 : 「田辺史、上毛野公、池原朝臣、住吉朝臣らの祖、思須美（しすみ）、和徳（わとく）の両人、大鷦鷯天皇（おおさざき-、[[仁徳天皇]]）御宇の年、百済国より化来す。しかして言うに、おのれらの祖、これ貴国将軍上野公竹合（たかはせ）なりといえり。天皇、矜憐して彼の族に混ず。しかして、この書に諸蕃人（= 渡来人）というなり。」
とあり<ref name="再発見">熊倉浩靖「上毛野国（かみつけのくに）から東国（あづま）へ」（『群馬史再発見』あさを社、2001年）。</ref>、田辺史・上毛野公・池原朝臣・住吉朝臣4氏が百済からの渡来人でありながら、竹葉瀬の後裔を仮冒したことがわかる。
== 考証 == 『日本書紀弘仁私記』にあるように、竹葉瀬を祖と仮冒する渡来人グループが形成されていた。ここから、上毛野氏が文化・軍事両面において対朝鮮の交渉に携わっていたことが示唆される<ref name="再発見"/>。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=竹葉瀬|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|竹葉瀬（古代氏族）|2010年}}}}
== 関連項目 == * [[上毛野氏]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:たかはせ}} [[Category:日本神話の人物]] [[Category:上毛野氏|伝たかはせ]] [[Category:古代日朝関係]]

He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who later fought against Silla.

The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>

<!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase-->
==Merged second translation== '''Takehase''' (竹葉瀬, also spelled 多奇波世 or 竹合) is a figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in texts such as the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}. He is sometimes referred to as "Kamitsukeno Takehase" or "Kamitsukeno Takeai," incorporating his clan name.
Takehase is described as a military commander and ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}, dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of Emperor {{ill|Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nintoku|qq=}} (16th Emperor of Japan).
== Historical Accounts == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}, in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku’s reign (traditional date), Takehase was sent to {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Silla|qq=}} to demand tribute, which Silla had not been offering. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before continuing his mission to Silla. Additionally, he is noted as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno no Tamichi|qq=}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans == The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen Shōjiroku|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=}} lists several clans as descendants of Takehase: * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Kamitsukeno no Ason''': Shared ancestry with the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|1=Shimotsukeno clan|lt=Shimotsukeno clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsukeno clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsukeno clan|qq=}}, descended from Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto's fifth-generation descendant, Takehase no Kimi. * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Sumiyoshi no Ason''': Shared ancestry with the Kamitsukeno clan, descended from Takehase no Kimi. * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Ikehara no Ason''': Related to the Sumiyoshi clan, descended from Takehase no Kimi. * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Kuwabara no Kimi''': Shared ancestry with the Kamitsukeno clan, descended from Takehase no Kimi. * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Kawai no Kimi''': Related to the Kamitsukeno clan, descended from Takehase no Kimi. * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Shōchō no Kubi''': Related to the Kamitsukeno clan, descended from Takehase no Kimi, through the third-generation descendant Kubi.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki Kōnin Shiki|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki Kōnin Shiki|qq=}} also states: > "The ancestors of the clans {{ill|Tanabe no Fuhito|ja|田辺史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabe no Fuhito|qq=}}, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara no Ason, and Sumiyoshi no Ason—namely, Susumi and Watoku—arrived from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baekje|qq=}} during the reign of Emperor {{ill|Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōjin|qq=}}. revision.comment: Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
'''Takahase''' (竹葉瀬) appears in ancient Japanese texts such as the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' as an individual from ancient [[Japan]]. Variants of the name include '''Takihase''' (多奇波世) and '''Takeai''' (竹合). He is also referred to with clan names such as ''Kamitsukeno Takahase'' and ''Kamitsukeno Takeai.''
He is noted as the ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}} and was said to be a general dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], the 16th emperor.
== Records ==
According to the ''Nihon Shoki,'' in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku's reign, Takahase was dispatched to [[:en:Silla|Silla]] to question their failure to pay tribute. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before proceeding to Silla. He is also recorded as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno Tamichi|qq=}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans ==
The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' lists the following clans as descendants of Takahase: * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kamitsukeno Ason: Common ancestor with the Shimotsukeno Ason. Descendants of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto, through Takihase no Kimi, fifth-generation descendant of Toyokuniwake no Mikoto. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Sumiyoshi Ason: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Ikehara Ason: Related to Sumiyoshi. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kuwabara Kō: Common ancestor with Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Kawai Kō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi. * Imperial Lineage, Sakyo, Akimachi Ō: Related to Kamitsukeno. Descendants of Takihase no Kimi and his grandson Kubi.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|qq=}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baekje|qq=}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|lang=ja|chapter=竹葉瀬|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|qq=}}|year=2010|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|竹葉瀬（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}

<!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase-->
==Merged second translation== '''Takehase''' (竹葉瀬, also spelled 多奇波世 or 竹合) is a figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in texts such as the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. He is sometimes referred to as "Kamitsukeno Takehase" or "Kamitsukeno Takeai," incorporating his clan name.
Takehase is described as a military commander and ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}, dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Nintoku]] (16th Emperor of Japan).
== Historical Accounts == According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku’s reign (traditional date), Takehase was sent to [[:en:Silla|Silla]] to demand tribute, which Silla had not been offering. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before continuing his mission to Silla. Additionally, he is noted as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno no Tamichi|qq=}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans == The [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] lists several clans as descendants of Takehase: * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Kamitsukeno no Ason''': Shared ancestry with the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|1=Shimotsukeno clan|lt=Shimotsukeno clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsukeno clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsukeno clan|qq=}}, descended from Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto's fifth-generation descendant, Takehase no Kimi. * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Sumiyoshi no Ason''': Shared ancestry with the Kamitsukeno clan, descended from Takehase no Kimi. * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Ikehara no Ason''': Related to the Sumiyoshi clan, descended from Takehase no Kimi. * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Kuwabara no Kimi''': Shared ancestry with the Kamitsukeno clan, descended from Takehase no Kimi. * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Kawai no Kimi''': Related to the Kamitsukeno clan, descended from Takehase no Kimi. * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Shōchō no Kubi''': Related to the Kamitsukeno clan, descended from Takehase no Kimi, through the third-generation descendant Kubi.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki Kōnin Shiki|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki Kōnin Shiki|qq=}} also states: > "The ancestors of the clans {{ill|Tanabe no Fuhito|ja|田辺史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabe no Fuhito|qq=}}, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara no Ason, and Sumiyoshi no Ason—namely, Susumi and Watoku—arrived from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] during the reign of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Ōjin]]. They claimed descent from the Japanese general Takehase no Kimi, and Emperor Nintoku allowed them to join the clan."
This indicates that groups of immigrants from Baekje falsely claimed descent from Takehase.<ref name="rediscovery">{{Cite book|title=Rediscovering Gunma’s History|author=Hiroyasu Kumakura|publisher=Asao-sha|year=2001}}</ref>
== Analysis == The formation of immigrant groups claiming descent from Takehase, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki Kōnin Shiki|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki Kōnin Shiki|qq=}}, suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played an active role in cultural and military diplomacy with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>

The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki Private Commentary (Kōnin Edition)|qq=}}'' includes the following note in its introduction: > "Tanabe no Fuhito, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara Ason, and Sumiyoshi Ason were descended from two individuals, Shisumi and Watoku, who came from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baekje|qq=}} during the reign of Emperor Ōsazaki (another name for Emperor Nintoku). They claimed that their ancestor was General Kamitsukeno Takeai. The emperor, out of pity, allowed them to integrate into his people, thus labeling them as ‘foreign clans’ in this text."
This passage highlights that the Tanabe, Kamitsukeno, Ikehara, and Sumiyoshi clans were originally immigrants from Baekje who falsely claimed descent from Takahase.<ref name="rediscovery">Kumakura Hiroyasu, "From Kamitsukeno Province to the East" (''Rediscovering Gunma History''), Asawo Publishing, 2001.</ref>
== Analysis ==
The presence of groups falsely claiming Takahase as their ancestor, as described in the ''Nihon Shoki Private Commentary,'' suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played significant roles in cultural and military interactions with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>

<!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase--><!--Takehase-->
==Merged second translation== '''Takehase''' (竹葉瀬, also spelled 多奇波世 or 竹合) is a figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in texts such as the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. He is sometimes referred to as "Kamitsukeno Takehase" or "Kamitsukeno Takeai," incorporating his clan name.
Takehase is described as a military commander and ancestor of the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}, dispatched to [[Korea]] during the reign of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Nintoku]] (16th Emperor of Japan).
== Historical Accounts == According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 53rd year of Emperor Nintoku’s reign (traditional date), Takehase was sent to [[:en:Silla|Silla]] to demand tribute, which Silla had not been offering. During his journey, he captured a white deer, which he presented to the emperor before continuing his mission to Silla. Additionally, he is noted as the elder brother of {{ill|Kamitsukeno no Tamichi|ja|上毛野田道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno no Tamichi|qq=}}, who later fought against Silla.
== Descendant Clans == The [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] lists several clans as descendants of Takehase: * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Kamitsukeno no Ason''': Shared ancestry with the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|下毛野氏|1=Shimotsukeno clan|lt=Shimotsukeno clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsukeno clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsukeno clan|qq=}}, descended from Toyokiiribiko no Mikoto's fifth-generation descendant, Takehase no Kimi. * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Sumiyoshi no Ason''': Shared ancestry with the Kamitsukeno clan, descended from Takehase no Kimi. * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Ikehara no Ason''': Related to the Sumiyoshi clan, descended from Takehase no Kimi. * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Kuwabara no Kimi''': Shared ancestry with the Kamitsukeno clan, descended from Takehase no Kimi. * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Kawai no Kimi''': Related to the Kamitsukeno clan, descended from Takehase no Kimi. * '''Imperial Lineage (Left Capital) Shōchō no Kubi''': Related to the Kamitsukeno clan, descended from Takehase no Kimi, through the third-generation descendant Kubi.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki Kōnin Shiki|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki Kōnin Shiki|qq=}} also states: > "The ancestors of the clans {{ill|Tanabe no Fuhito|ja|田辺史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabe no Fuhito|qq=}}, Kamitsukeno no Kimi, Ikehara no Ason, and Sumiyoshi no Ason—namely, Susumi and Watoku—arrived from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] during the reign of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Ōjin]]. They claimed descent from the Japanese general Takehase no Kimi, and Emperor Nintoku allowed them to join the clan."
This indicates that groups of immigrants from Baekje falsely claimed descent from Takehase.<ref name="rediscovery">{{Cite book|title=Rediscovering Gunma’s History|author=Hiroyasu Kumakura|publisher=Asao-sha|year=2001}}</ref>
== Analysis == The formation of immigrant groups claiming descent from Takehase, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki Kōnin Shiki|ja|日本書紀弘仁私記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki Kōnin Shiki|qq=}}, suggests that the Kamitsukeno clan played an active role in cultural and military diplomacy with Korea.<ref name="rediscovery"/>
